Tafseer: The Qur'an is its own proof of truth: 'Alif-Lam-Ra. These are the verses of the Book and a clear Qur'an.' We have conducted a comprehensive discussion on the abbreviated letters (Huroof-e-Muqatta'at) at the beginning of the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. In our view, 'Alif-Lam-Ra' is the Qur'anic name of this Surah, and 'tilka' (these/those) points toward it. We have also discussed the word 'Al-Kitab' at the beginning of the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. It refers to the divine Book which Allah promised to reveal and of which previous prophets gave glad tidings. The indefiniteness (tanween) of the word 'Qur'an' is for expressing its high status and grandeur. The meaning is that this discourse which is being recited to them comprises the verses of the divine Book and a clear Qur'an. It contains the proof of its truth within itself; it is not in need of any external proof. Despite this, those who are not believing in it, and are demanding miracles to test its truth, are in reality inviting their own doom.

'Perhaps those who disbelieve will wish that they had been Muslims.' That is, today these people are arrogantly and haughtily denying this Book, but times will come ahead, and come repeatedly, when they will wish that they had accepted this Book and become Muslims so that they would have been saved from these terrifying consequences. This theme has also passed in Surah Ibrahim, verse 44.

'Leave them to eat and enjoy themselves and be diverted by [false] hope, for they are going to know.' That is, if they are lost in their self-indulgences, paying no heed to you and your call (addressing the Prophet, peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), then leave them to their state. Let them eat, drink, enjoy themselves for a few days, and dream of sweet hopes; very soon that time is coming when they will see the consequence of their self-indulgence with their own eyes.

Commentary on Verses 4 and 5: The Reason for Delaying the Divine Punishment: 'And We destroyed not any city but that for it was a known decree. No nation will precede its term, nor will they delay it.' This explains the reason why the punishment is delayed. The meaning is: despite their demand, the reason why the punishment is not coming upon them is that this delay is based on a sunnah (established way) of Allah. The divine sunnah is that He does not send punishment upon a nation until He fully establishes His argument against them. Even after this completion of proof, if the nation does not desist from its rebellion, it is bound to meet its end. The completion of proof and the limit of moral decline at which a nation becomes deserving of punishment are recorded in a divine decree. When the term of this decree is complete, the nation is destroyed. Not a hair's breadth of difference occurs in this; there is neither advancement nor delay.

Commentary on Verses 6 and 7: 'And they said, "O you upon whom the message has been sent down, indeed you are mad. Why do you not bring us the angels, if you should be of the truthful?"'

Addressing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) sarcastically, they would say: 'O you who claim that a reminder has been revealed to him from God for us, you seem to us to be a madman, as you are threatening us with punishment and claiming success and salvation for yourself, whereas our conditions are a thousand times better than your conditions and those of your companions. If we are despised and rejected by God, and you are beloved and favored by God, then why have we been given these blessings and why are you deprived of them? This situation clearly shows that you are a madman and are talking like madmen. And it is also part of your madness that you claim an angel comes to you; if angels do come and you are truthful in this claim, why do you not bring these angels to us so that we may also see and hear what they are like and what they say? After all, what special feather do you have in your cap that they come to you and do not come to us?'

In 'Law ma ta'teena', 'law ma' is used in the sense of urging, provoking, or demanding, meaning: 'Why do you not do this?' Precedents of this exist in Arabic speech and the Holy Qur'an.

'We do not send down the angels except with truth, and the disbelievers would not then be reprieved.' This is the reply to the aforementioned demand. 'Truth' (Al-Haqq) here means 'decision'. The meaning is: Allah wants people to use their intellect and understanding, ponder over the signs that exist in the cosmos and the soul to which the Qur'an is drawing attention, and believe in their light. They should not demand the descent of angels to believe. We send angels down upon people only when people become blind and deaf and nothing remains for them except punishment. At that time, the angels come with God's decision, and that nation is completely destroyed. After that, no one gets any respite.

God Himself is Responsible for the Preservation of the Qur'an: 'Indeed, it is We who sent down the message [i.e., the Quran] and indeed, We will be its guardian.' This is a very powerful reassurance and consolation offered to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)—showing that if these people (the Quraysh) are not appreciating this great Qur'an, do not grieve over it. This Book has been revealed to you by Us, without any demand or desire from your side, and We alone are its guardians. If these people reject it, let them reject it; God will raise others for it who will accept it and will not hesitate to make any sacrifice in the way of its call and preservation. This very theme has been stated in Surah Al-An'am: 'But if these disbelieve in it, We have already entrusted it to a people who are not therein disbelievers.' The meaning is: if these people accept it, it is for their own success in this world and the Hereafter; and if they, due to their misfortune and bad deeds, reject it, others will step forward to accept it. Consequently, when the Quraysh refused to accept it, Allah opened the hearts of the Ansar for it; they embraced it warmly and did not hesitate to make any sacrifice in the way of its preservation. In 'Indeed, it is We who sent down' (Inna nahnu nazzalna), the double emphasis (hasr) is intended to express the reality that you have not received this Book from Us by asking for it, such that the responsibility of making people accept it lies upon you. Your responsibility is solely to convey and call; you perform that. As for the preservation of this Book and its establishment and survival, that belongs to Us; We will arrange for its preservation, establishment, and survival. In what ways Allah fulfilled this promise—history contains the complete answer to this question. There is no occasion to go into those details here.

Commentary on Verses 10 to 13: Reference to the Stories of the Prophets: To further emphasize and prove the theme of consolation and solace, this refers to the stories of the prophets (peace be upon them)—showing that the experience you are facing today from your nation is nothing new. We have already sent messengers before you among the previous nations, and they too had to face the same conditions from their respective nations. Just as you are being mocked today, the messengers who came before you were also mocked. The call of God's messengers is such that criminals drowned in disobedience do not tolerate it quietly; it pricks them like arrows and needles, and they exert all their power to suppress it. Consequently, these people of your nation will also not believe in it; rather, they have adopted the very path that their predecessors adopted, and they will surely face the same end that their predecessors faced.

Commentary on Verses 14 and 15: The Actual Cause of the Deniers' Denial: 'And [even] if We opened to them a gate from the heaven and they continued therein to ascend, they would say, "Our eyes have only been dazzled. Rather, we are a people affected by magic."' That is, far from sending down angels, even if We opened a gate in the heaven for them and they began to ascend into it, they would still find some excuse not to believe. They would say: 'Our vision has been dazzled, and our entire nation, men and women alike, has been affected by magic.' The meaning is: the actual cause of their not believing is not that they are not being shown a miracle of their choice; rather, the actual cause is that they are not ready to accept any change in their desires and assumptions, and due to their arrogance, they are not ready to listen to the Prophet.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 16 to 25: Indication of the Signs of the Cosmos and the Soul: In the following, attention is drawn to those signs which are spread in every corner of the cosmos, the soul, heaven, and earth, and which confirm the things to which the Prophet is calling. The purpose of mentioning these signs is that in the presence of nature's miracles, where remains the need for any new miracle? If there are seeing eyes and thinking brains, every single leaf is a volume of the Creator's recognition.

Commentary on Verses 16 to 18: 'And We have indeed placed constellations in the heaven and have beautified it for the observers. And We have guarded it from every accursed devil - Except one who steals a hearing, then he is pursued by a clear burning flame.'

Indication of the Heavenly Signs: 'Buruj' means fortresses and palaces. Here, it refers to those heavenly fortresses that God has made in the heavens, in which His forces of angels and archangels are constantly stationed, guarding those limits and spheres beyond which neither the devils among humans nor the devils among jinn are allowed to advance. And if any devil attempts to steal a hearing of the matters of the Exalted Assembly, a shining flame pursues him immediately. The meaning is: just as soldiers stationed on the towers of a fortress keep a strict watch over the enemy's men, not allowing any stranger to cross their borders, similarly, God's appointed angels target with piercing flames those devils among jinn who attempt to intrude and spy within their borders. By the way, this completely demolishes the foundation of soothsaying (kahanat) which was highly prevalent in the days of ignorance, and under the cover of which soothsayers claimed knowledge of the unseen to fool the simple-minded public. The Qur'an clarified that devils have no access to the spheres of the Exalted Assembly, and if they attempt to listen secretly, they become targets of the pelting of flames. From this aspect, the attribute 'rajeem' (accursed/pelted) is used for the devil, because rajeem means 'pelted with stones', and the word 'all' (kull) expresses the reality that even the greatest devil cannot enter the limits secured by God.

Whatever modern science might explain about what is commonly called falling stars, it does not refute this reality described by the Qur'an, because science's access is only to the outward causes of things. What tasks divine decree performs through these flames is beyond the reach of science to explain. Only Allah can explain it, and the sole means of knowing His matters is divine revelation and the Qur'an.

This subject has come here incidentally; the actual purpose of the verse, as we indicated above, is to draw attention to those signs of heaven and earth which are spread in every corner of this universe, and each of these signs confirms what the Qur'an and the Prophet are informing about. The meaning is: for those who possess the capability to think and understand, the heaven is full of signs and so is the earth; but those whose eyes are closed, instead of obtaining guidance from these signs, demand miracles, and even if the greatest miracle were shown to them, they would still remain blind, as indicated above.

From the perspective of the coherence of speech, special attention must be paid to the phrase 'and have beautified it for the observers'. The meaning is: if someone's eyes are open, there is no shortage of miracles for him in this world. Let him look at the heaven. How has nature adorned it with stars, planets, the moon, the sun, twilight, rainbows, and countless other diverse wonders, such that wherever the gaze is lifted, man is left astonished and exclaims: 'Our Lord, You did not create this aimlessly.' This is not a plaything of an amusement-seeker or something that came into existence as an accidental happening; rather, it is a world created by a Wise, All-Knowing Being Who possesses endless power and wisdom. It is the necessary requirement of His power and wisdom that He does not just create it and leave it aimlessly; rather, it is necessary that He bring a Day in which He gathers everyone to account for their good and bad deeds, and rewards or punishes them according to their deeds.

Indication of the Signs of the Earth: 'And the earth - We have spread it out and cast therein firmly set mountains and grown therein of everything well-balanced.'

After drawing attention to the signs of the heaven, attention is drawn to the signs of the earth—that just as the heaven is full of God's signs, the earth is also full of His signs. The heaven is above like a canopy, shining, and it is the power and wisdom of this Powerful, Self-Sustaining God that He has cast the anchors of mountains within the earth to maintain its balance; otherwise, as stated in another place, it would have tilted to one side along with all creatures. 'And grown therein of everything well-balanced' points to the reality that whatever God has created in it, He has created with a specific balance and proportion, and it is by the blessing of this balance and proportion that the earth has become suitable for human residence, civilization, and livelihood. Otherwise, as stated ahead, there was no shortage of anything in God's treasuries; if He had left anything to exceed its required and determined limit, the entire system of this earth would have been disrupted, and instead of humans, some other creature would have inhabited it, or it would have remained completely uninhabited.

Indication of the System of Sustenance (Rububiyyah): 'And We have made for you therein means of livelihood and [for] those for whom you are not providers.' According to the well-known rule of the language, the preposition has been omitted in one place: 'wa man lastum' is actually 'wa li-man lastum'.

After drawing attention to the signs of heaven and earth, Allah has pointed to His system of sustenance: in this earth, He has created for man all those things of necessity which he requires for the survival of his life and his comfort, and He has also arranged the provisions for those creatures whose sustenance is not the responsibility of man, even though they are useful to man and man benefits from them in various ways.

Indication of Balance and Proportion: 'And there is not a thing but that with Us are its depositories, and We do not send it down except in a known measure.' As we indicated above, this points to the balance and proportion present in all the works of the Creator of the universe, upon which depends the establishment and survival of this world. God does not lack anything; He has the depositories of everything, but the survival of this world requires that everything manifest with a proportion. For this reason, He sends everything only in that measure in which its sending is necessary. If anything exceeds its required limit even slightly—and this excess too, when it happens, happens only by God's command—it results in great calamities in this world. All these things prove that this world has neither come into existence as an accidental happening, as atheists and deniers think, nor is it a playground of various gods and goddesses, as the polytheists assumed. Consequently, in verse 2 of Surah Al-Furqan, this proportion of the components of the universe is termed as 'measure' (taqdeer), and this measure is presented as a clear proof of monotheism and the negation of polytheism. Its details, if Allah wills, will come in their respective place.

Further Explanation of the System of Sustenance: 'And We have sent the fertilizing winds and sent down water from the sky and given you drink from it. And you are not its treasurers.' 'Laqaha' means to fertilize and make pregnant. From this comes 'laqihah' and its plural 'lawaqih'. 'Riyah' refers to the winds that fertilize the clouds and cause rain.

This is a further explanation of His sustenance to which Allah pointed above—showing that water, which is the source of life for you and all living creatures, and the medium for bringing all means of livelihood into existence, is obtained by you only by God's command and His mercy. He is the One Who sends the seasonal winds that gather the clouds and fertilize them, from which you receive water to drink. It was not in your power to store this water and make it rain whenever you needed; it is God's arrangement and His mercy that He has made this arrangement for you.

'And indeed, it is We who give life and cause death, and We are the Inheritor.' This clarifies the necessary implication of the matters stated above—that We alone grant life, We alone cause death, and We alone are the Inheritor of everything. Just as no one has any share in life and death, no one has any share in the inheritance of the earth and its inhabitants. The return and ultimate master of all is Allah alone. If anyone has pinned hopes on anyone else, it is merely an illusion holding no reality.

Commentary on Verses 24 and 25: 'And We have already known the predecessor [generations] among you, and We have already known the successor [generations]. And indeed, your Lord - He will gather them; indeed, He is Wise and Knowing.' That is, let no one remain under the misunderstanding as to whose power it is to keep the account of such countless creatures. It is stated that We alone created everyone and provided for everyone; We know those who have passed away, and We are also aware of those who came later or will come. Allah will gather all of them and keep their account. He is Wise and Knowing. His being Wise requires that He bring a Day of recompense, and His being Knowing means that no one can be hidden from Him, nor is He unaware of any deed of anyone.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 26 to 48: Reference to the Story of Adam and Iblees: In the subsequent verses, reference is made to the initial story of Adam and Iblees. The purpose of this reference is to clarify that those who are not believing today, and are denying God's Messenger with great pride and arrogance, their disbelief and arrogance are not due to a lack of arguments proving the truth of the messengers' words, or because they are not being shown miracles of their choice; rather, these are people who have fallen into the deception of Iblees—the threat of which Iblees had made when he was commanded to prostrate to Adam. At that time, refusing to prostrate, he had said: 'If I am granted respite, I will surely mislead all the offspring of Adam; only those will escape my trap who are Your sincere servants.' Allah granted respite to Satan and said: 'Go, whoever among them falls into your trap, ensnare them; but you will have no power over My sincere servants.' Read the subsequent verses in this light:

'And We did certainly create man from clay of altered black mud.'

'Salasal' refers to dry clay which, when dry, makes a clinking sound. 'Hama'in masnoon' refers to black, smelling mud.

The life of all living creatures on earth began from water and mud; then, for those among them whom He willed to live on dry land, Allah provided dry land, and within this dry land, those powers and capabilities emerged which Allah had placed within them and which required the specific environment of the earth for their development. Man is also not excluded from this general rule; he too is a creature of water, mud, and dry land. However, in contrast to other living creatures, as mentioned ahead, he has this distinction that Allah fashioned him (taswiyah)—that is, adorned him with all those powers and capabilities necessary for the completion of his purpose of creation. Here, keep in view that it is not merely said that man was created from dust, as in other places, but with it 'altered black mud' is also added. The purpose of this is to point to the starting point of life and to draw attention to the reality that one whose life began from such a humble element should under no circumstances act arrogantly toward the Self-Sustaining, All-Powerful Creator Who created him from such a humble element and then adorned him with highly elevated capabilities.

'And the jinn We created before from scorching fire.' 'Samoom' refers to hot wind and flames of heat.

After the creation of humans, this informs of the creation of the jinn—showing that Allah created the jinn before humans, and their creation was from the subtle element of fire, i.e., hot wind. Keep in view that the primary purpose of the Qur'an in describing the creation of humans and jinn here is not to trace their physical elements, but the actual purpose is to clarify that cause of hostility between man and Iblees which is mentioned in the subsequent verses. Iblees, who was from the jinn, made this physical creation the argument for his superiority and refused to prostrate to Adam, due to which he became accursed; and this accursedness became a permanent cause of hostility and jealousy between his offspring and the offspring of Adam. For this reason, the Qur'an has referred to it.

Commentary on Verses 28 and 29: 'And [remember] when your Lord said to the angels, "I am creating a human being from clay of altered black mud. So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My soul, then fall down to him in prostration."

Now the actual subject is being described, for which the above point was mentioned as an introduction. We have discussed this story of Adam and Iblees in detail under verses 33-38 of Surah Al-Baqarah and verses 11-25 of Surah Al-A'raf. Repeating it here would cause unnecessary length. However, we will point here to those matters necessary for understanding the sequence of speech.

The Basis of Human Honor: One thing learned from these verses is that Allah commanded the angels to prostrate to Adam even before Adam's physical creation was complete. The second thing learned is that alongside this command of prostration, there was also a clarification from Allah that this prostration should be performed when He had proportioned Adam—that is, adorned him with all powers and capabilities—and breathed His soul into him. From this, it becomes clear on its own that whatever honor and excellence man possesses is entirely by virtue of those powers, capabilities, and the divine soul that have been entrusted to him. If man guards and develops them, he is worthy of the angels' prostration; and if he destroys them, then he is not only an animal but even lower than animals. 'I breathed into him of My soul' shows that a divine light has also been entrusted within man, which is the source of all his spiritual perfections, and through it he connects with God, provided he guards it fully. If he loses it, he is like a house without a lamp, inside of which there is only darkness.

Commentary on Verses 30 and 31: 'So the angels prostrated - all of them entirely. Except Iblees; he refused to be with those who prostrated.' All the angels, and all creatures included under their command, obeyed this command of Allah and prostrated to Adam; but Iblees, who was from the jinn, refused to obey this command. The reason for this refusal, as detailed ahead, he stated solely as: because he was created from fire, he was a superior creature, so how could he prostrate to a human who was created from clay and was inferior to him?

Commentary on Verses 32 to 35: '[Allah] said, "O Iblees, what is [the matter] with you that you are not with those who prostrated?" He said, "Never would I prostrate to a human whom You created from clay of altered black mud." [Allah] said, "Then get out of it, for indeed, you are accursed. And indeed, upon you is the curse until the Day of Recompense."

This command was clearly a test from Allah for the angels and the jinn, in which the angels succeeded, but Iblees, who was from the jinn, failed due to his pride and arrogance. Allah, as a punishment for this rebellion of his, declared him accursed and rejected, commanding him to get out from there, and stated that this curse will remain imposed upon him until the Day of Recompense, after which he will face the actual punishment for this crime of his.

Commentary on Verses 36 to 38: 'He said, "My Lord, then reprieve me until the Day they are resurrected." [Allah] said, "Then indeed, you are of those reprieved. Until the Day of the well-known time."' Instead of being affected or humbled by this command of expulsion and curse, Iblees became even more arrogant. He demanded respite until the Day of Resurrection so that he might be given the opportunity to subject Adam and his offspring to his trials, proving that they are not worthy of the honor bestowed upon them and that he was completely in the right in refusing to prostrate to them. Allah accepted his request and granted him this respite until the Promised Day of Resurrection.

Commentary on Verses 39 and 40: '[Iblees] said, "My Lord, because You have put me in error, I will surely make [disobedience] attractive to them on earth, and I will mislead them all. Except, among them, Your chosen servants."' 'Ighwa' means to mislead. Since Iblees considered himself in the right in refusing to prostrate to Adam, he insolently attributed the act of 'misleading' (ighwa) to Allah Himself. The meaning is: if I became misled due to non-compliance with this command, it is not my fault; rather, this command itself was such that I could not comply with it, so if I became misled because of it, You forced me into it. 'I will surely make [disobedience] attractive to them on earth'—that is, I will make the world and its desires look beautiful in man's eyes, and he will get so entangled in their love that neither will he remember that he was the one to whom angels prostrated, nor will he have any concern for the Hereafter, nor will he remain established on Your monotheism. 'And I will mislead them all'—that is, I will turn them away from the straight path of monotheism and make them wander in the valleys of polytheism. The statement of Iblees is quoted in Surah Al-A'raf, verses 16-17 thus: 'He said, "Because You have put me in error, I will surely sit in wait for them on Your straight path. Then I will come to them from before them and from behind them and on their right and on their left, and You will not find most of them grateful."' From this, we learn that the specific target of Satan's misleading is monotheism. All his temptations and plots are for this single purpose: to turn the offspring of Adam away from the straight path leading to God and trap them in the misguidance of polytheism. 'Except, among them, Your chosen servants'—that is, if some fortunate ones among Your servants escape my temptations, only those will escape whom You have chosen for Yourself to remain established on Your monotheism and remembrance of the Hereafter; the rest will all become targets of my misguidance.

'[Allah] said, "This is a path [leading] to Me which is straight."' Allah said that this path of monotheism, from which you are threatening to mislead the children of Adam, is not a crooked or winding path; rather, it is an extremely straight and level path that leads to Me. My servants who want to reach Me, if they adopt this path, it will automatically lead them to My threshold; there is no fear of deviation or misguidance in it. Only the doomed will abandon this straight path to adopt a crooked one.

Here, 'alayya' (upon Me) which has come after 'sirat' (path) is according to a specific style of the Arabic language. In Arabic, this style of expression exists to praise a straight path: it shows that this path is so straight that it leads the traveler directly to the destination. Quoting precedents of Arabic speech would cause unnecessary length, so we content ourselves with presenting some parallels from the Qur'an. In Surah Hud: 'Indeed, I have relied upon Allah, my Lord and your Lord. There is no creature but that He holds its forelock. Indeed, my Lord is on a straight path.' 'Indeed, my Lord is on a straight path'—meaning, to find Him and reach Him, I do not have to traverse many crooked valleys; to find Him and reach Him, it is sufficient that I remain established and firm on the highway of monotheism, busy in His worship, and trust in Him that He will help me in every difficulty. Similarly, in verse 9 of Surah An-Nahl: 'And upon Allah is the direction of the [straight] path, and among them [i.e., the paths] are those that are deviant. And if He willed, He could have guided you all.'

'Indeed, My servants - no authority will you have over them, except those who follow you of the deviators.'

The Limit of Iblees's Respite: 'Sultan' means authority and power. Allah also made it clear to Iblees that the respite you are receiving is only to the extent that you can tempt people to walk on your path and try to mislead them. This respite does not mean that you have been given the power to put whoever you want on the path of misguidance. Your power will be active only upon those who choose to follow you and prefer misguidance. You will have no power over My servants who remain safe from your trials and walk on the straight path shown by Me, contrary to all your temptations.

Commentary on Verses 43 and 44: 'And indeed, Hell is the promised place for them all. It has seven gates; for every gate is of them a portion designated.' This describes the end of those who follow Satan. It is said that all those who abandon My straight path to choose the path of Iblees—whether they are from the jinn or from humans, and no matter how great their number—their abode will be Hell. They will face that punishment of which they are being informed today. To understand the emphasis of the word 'ajma'een' (all together), keep in view Iblees's statement 'I will mislead them all'. Iblees claimed with great pride that he would mislead everyone. In response, he received a full reply from Allah: if you mislead everyone, then We too will leave no stone unturned in throwing all of them into Hell. 'It has seven gates' describes the vastness of Hell, and if you consider, there is also a subtle indication in this that the destructive sins which ruin man and put him on the path of Hell are basically seven in their origin. Satan traps man in one of these, or ultimately in all of them, to put him on the path of Hell.

From the perspective of the coherence of speech, the obvious meaning of the verse is: let no one remain under the misunderstanding as to where such a vast Hell will be provided from to punish such countless and endless creatures, encompassing all of them. God has prepared a very vast Hell to punish such criminals, which will have seven gates, and from each gate, separate groups of the people of Hell will enter. The words 'portion designated' (juz'um maqsoom) indicate that there will be a specific categorization among those who enter through the different gates of Hell. On what basis this categorization will be made is difficult to say definitively, since there is no explicit clarification in the Qur'an itself. But our mind repeatedly goes to the idea that if the things mentioned by the Qur'an as fundamental destructive sins are counted, they fall under seven headings, and they are: 1. Polytheism, 2. Severing of kinship ties, 3. Murder, 4. Fornication, 5. False testimony, 6. Oppressing the weak, 7. Rebellion. Under each of these are long details, which is not the place to mention here. A detailed discussion on them, if Allah wills, will come under verses 22 to 38 of Surah Bani Israel.

Commentary on Verses 45 to 48: 'Indeed, the righteous will be within gardens and springs. [They will be told], "Enter it in peace, safe." And We will have removed whatever is within their breasts of resentment, [as] brothers on thrones facing each other. No fatigue will touch them therein, nor from it will they [ever] be extinguished [i.e., removed].'

The Good End of the God-Fearing: After describing the end of the people of Hell, this describes the end of those who fear God and act upon His commands. It is said that they will be within gardens and springs, enjoying the comfort of eternal life. For them will be this glad tiding from God: 'Enter it in peace, safe.' Now, remain in it completely free from all kinds of worry and anxiety, and enjoy the eternal blessings of your Lord. Their hearts will be completely clean toward one another; for this reason, like brothers, they will be seated on the thrones of Paradise facing each other. Those who possess personal resentments, even if they gather in a assembly, sit turning away from each other; but among those who fear God, firstly, personal resentments do not exist, and if some unpleasantness arose due to any difference in interpretation, opinion, or taste, that too will be removed after the uncovering of reality, and Allah will cleanse their hearts from its effects, and they will become completely united and close to each other. 'No fatigue will touch them therein'—there will be the best life of comfort, and at every moment, such diversity and variety will be present in God's blessings that the people of Paradise will never grow tired of it. 'Nor from it will they [ever] be removed' is the glad tiding of eternity, and this is the greatest glad tiding. Even if one obtains the best life of comfort, but with it remains the fear that it is temporary or fleeting, all the enjoyment is spoiled. The people of Paradise will be completely safe from this fear. The life of comfort they receive will never be snatched from them.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 49 to 79: Warning to the Arrogant of the Quraysh: First, there is a short introduction in the subsequent verses, clarifying that no one should be deceived by God's delay. He is highly Forgiving and Merciful, and also highly Retributive and Dominant. Then, those arrogant of the Quraysh who were demanding to see the angels are warned: the coming of angels is no simple matter. Whenever they come, they come only for a major task. To clarify this reality in the light of history, the story of the people of Lot is narrated, whose destroyed towns the Quraysh frequently passed by during their trade journeys. The purpose of narrating this story is to inform the Quraysh: if you desire to see the angels, be prepared also to face the same end that the people of Lot faced. Read the subsequent verses in this light:

Commentary on Verses 49 and 50: '[O Muhammad], inform My servants that it is I who am the Forgiving, the Merciful, And that My punishment - it is the painful punishment.'

The Correct Concept Regarding Allah: These verses clarify the correct concept regarding Allah—showing that no one should fall into the misunderstanding due to His delay that the good and bad are equal in His sight. He is highly Merciful, and also highly Retributive and Dominant. Those who walk on His straight path have to pass through trials to test their patience, but ultimately they are the ones who succeed. In contrast, those who deviate from His path receive respite for a while so that the proof may be fully established against them, but ultimately they face the punishment for their deviation.

Commentary on Verses 51 to 53: 'And inform them about the guests of Abraham, When they entered upon him and said, "Peace." He said, "Indeed, we are afraid of you." They said, "Fear not. Indeed, we give you good tidings of a knowing boy."

A Testimony of History: In support of the aforementioned reality, this presents a testimony of history—showing that the very same angels who came to Abraham to give glad tidings of a wise son, also came to the people of Lot with the bolt of punishment. 'He said, "Indeed, we are afraid of you"'—this statement of Abraham to the angels is proof that he was aware of the reality that the public arrival of angels in this manner could only be for a major task; for this reason, he felt anxious at their arrival, and expressed his anxiety before them. The people of Lot were nearby, and their moral corruption had reached its ultimate limit; for this reason, seeing the angels, Abraham must have felt that the doom of the people of Lot had arrived, and these angels were the precursor of their doom. The angels comforted Abraham: 'Do not feel anxious; as far as you are concerned, we have come to give you glad tidings of a wise son.' By using the attribute 'wise' (aleem) for the son, the angels also made a subtle indication that the son would not merely be a son, but would also be blessed with the knowledge of prophethood. Clearly, this increased the value of the glad tiding immensely. The greatest thing Abraham could desire for his son was that he be a prophet.

'He said, "Have you given me good tidings although old age has prevented me? Then of what [kind of child] do you give good tidings?"' Abraham's Graceful Inquiry: How happy Abraham must have been upon hearing the glad tidings of a wise son is obvious, but since the manifestation of this glad tiding was highly unlikely under outward physical conditions, he wanted to obtain further confirmation. Consequently, he said in a very beautiful manner: 'Are you giving me this glad tiding when old age has overtaken me? Then on what basis are you giving this glad tiding?' The meaning is: if this glad tiding is from Allah, let it be explicitly stated.

'They said, "We have given you good tidings in truth, so do not be of the despairing."' 'Truth' (Al-Haqq) here means a reality that is bound to happen.

Assurance from the Angels: The angels assured Abraham: 'Do not be disheartened and despairing due to unfavorable outward conditions; the glad tidings we have given you are of a reality, and no matter how unfavorable the outward conditions are, this matter is bound to happen.' Implicit in this reply of the angels is that this glad tiding is from Allah, and when a matter is from Allah, the question of favorable or unfavorable conditions does not arise, for Allah is powerful over everything.

'He said, "And who despairs of the mercy of his Lord except for those astray?"' When Abraham was assured that this glad tiding was indeed from Allah, these words came spontaneously from his tongue, expressing both the gratitude that arose within him after receiving assurance regarding this glad tiding, and the fact that the question he asked the angels was not out of despair of God's grace and mercy, but merely due to unfavorable outward causes. At the same time, he also expressed the reality that those who despair of God's mercy even in the most difficult circumstances are deprived of faith and astray.

'He said, "Then what is your business [here], O messengers?"' The word 'khatb' is mostly used for an important matter or a major task.

Upon hearing this glad tiding, Abraham became satisfied regarding his own matter, but an anxiety arose in his heart: the coming of an entire group of angels in this manner could not be merely to convey the glad tidings of a child's birth. Their arrival in this form must surely be for some important purpose. Moreover, the moral corruption of the nearby people of Lot had reached its ultimate limit, and Abraham was not unaware of their conditions; for this reason, he might have felt that this bolt of punishment was about to fall upon that crop of corruption. Consequently, he asked the angels: 'I have heard the glad tidings of a child's birth; now tell me, for what major task have you been sent at this time?'

Commentary on Verses 58 to 60: 'They said, "Indeed, we have been sent to a people of criminals, Except the family of Lot; indeed, we will save them all. Except his wife; we [i.e., Allah] decreed that she is of those who remain behind."' The angels replied that they had been sent to a nation of criminals. Two things emerge from this style of expression. One is that it expresses the anger and wrath that the angels held against the people of Lot. Consequently, instead of saying 'We have been sent to the people of Lot,' they said 'We have been sent to a nation of criminals.' This shows that they held such intense hatred against this nation that they did not even like to take its name.

The second is that it shows the reality that a decisive punishment from Allah falls upon a nation only when the entire society becomes corrupt, and moral corruption overcomes them in such a way that either no good person is left among them, or if a few individuals exist, their lives become miserable in that corrupt environment. 'Except the family of Lot; indeed, we will save them all.' Since the word 'Aal' (family) also includes followers, it is not unlikely that some people had believed in Lot (peace be upon him). The angels found it necessary to make this clarification regarding Lot and his followers to Abraham because, upon hearing the decision of punishment for the people of Lot, anxiety naturally arose in Abraham's heart as to what would happen to Lot and his followers. The angels removed his anxiety by stating that they would save all of them. 'Except his wife; we decreed that she is of those who remain behind'—the angels excluded only Lot's wife from this salvation. Regarding her, they said: 'We have designated her to remain with those who face the punishment, and she will suffer the same punishment.' This clarification made it clear that God's law is completely unbiased; to the extent that even a prophet's wife is not saved from its grip. Rather, if you keep in view the correct meaning of 'We decreed' (qaddarna), it also indicates that despite having a relationship with prophets and righteous people, if any person—whether man or woman—chooses the path of evil, they become even more deserving of God's wrath than others.

Commentary on Verses 61 and 62: 'So when the messengers came to the family of Lot, He said, "Indeed, you are a people unknown."' After giving glad tidings to Abraham, these angels arrived at Lot's place. Lot saw them and felt that these people did not belong to this area; so who are they, where have they come from, and for what purpose? Ultimately, he said: 'You seem to be strangers.' Meaning: 'I do not recognize you; please introduce yourselves.'

Commentary on Verses 63 and 64: 'They said, "Nay, we have come to you with that about which they were disputing, And we have come to you with truth, and indeed, we are truthful."' The angels said: 'We are not humans, as you have assumed; rather, we have come with that punishment with which you have been warning the people of your nation, but they kept disputing about it.' 'Truth' (Al-Haqq) here, as in verse 8, means 'punishment' and 'reality'. After stating that they had come with the punishment, there was no need to state explicitly that they were angels. 'And indeed, we are truthful' is by way of warning and confirmation—showing that do not fall into any misunderstanding by assuming us to be humans. What we are saying is bound to happen, so act upon the instructions we are giving without any delay or hesitation.

'"So set out with your family during a portion of the night and follow their backs and let not any among you look back and keep going to where you are commanded."' These are the instructions the angels gave to Lot (peace be upon him) on this occasion. The first instruction was: 'Set out from here with your family during the night and go to where you are commanded.' The reason for this instruction, as detailed ahead, was that in the morning, divine punishment was about to fall upon this town. The second instruction was: 'You walk behind all of them, just as a shepherd walks behind his flock, so that no one wanders away from the flock to become a prey of the wolf.' The third instruction was: 'After leaving the town, none of you should look back.' Looking back at a place while leaving it is a sign of attachment and affection for it. This attachment and affection under normal circumstances is a natural thing, and a natural requirement cannot be stopped or suppressed; but when leaving a town for which the decision of divine punishment has been made after the completion of proof, the believers must leave in such a way that no trace of attachment to it remains in the heart. It is narrated regarding the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that whenever he had to pass by the towns of punished nations, he would pass quickly and direct others to do the same.

'And We conveyed to him that decree - that the root of those would be cut off by early morning.' The preposition 'ila' (to) after 'qadayna' (We decreed) is proof that 'ablagna' (We conveyed) or a similar word is omitted here. The meaning is: We decided this matter and informed him that as soon as morning arrived, the root of these people would be cut off.

'And the people of the city came rejoicing.' 'City' (Madinah) here refers to Sodom, the town of Lot's people. When the wicked and corrupt of Sodom came to know that some handsome guests had arrived at Lot's house, they came rejoicing to his house, thinking that today a good prey has come to hand, and they would satisfy their desires today.

Commentary on Verses 68 and 69: 'He said, "Indeed, these are my guests, so do not shameful me. And fear Allah and do not disgrace me."' When Lot (peace be upon him) saw that the mob had attacked his house, and his honor and the honor of his guests were in danger, he appealed to them very effectively, mentioning both his honor and the fear of God: 'These people are my guests; guarding their honor is my moral duty. If you commit any excess against them, I will be completely disgraced in their eyes; they will say: "What a house we came to as guests, that he handed us over to the mob!"' Then he also reminded them of the fear of God: 'Fear Allah, and do not disgrace me by insulting my guests, lest I become unable to show my face to anyone.' This appeal, if the addressees had possessed any spark of moral sense, would have been highly effective, because respecting guests is considered a high virtue even among the worst of people; but what effect can it have on those whose moral sense is completely dead?

'They said, "Have we not forbidden you from [protecting] people?"'

Even the worst of people find some excuse for their misdeeds. Consequently, these wicked ones also created a political justification for their action: 'You conspire with outsiders. Outsiders come to you and you meet them. This conspiracy of yours can become a danger for us one day, so we are not ready to be deceived that you bring trouble upon us one day under the guise of guests.'

'He said, "These are my daughters, if you would be doing [so]."' The Final Measure to Awaken the Nation's Moral Sense: This was the final weapon Lot used to awaken the moral sense of these wicked ones. When he saw that these base people were not going to desist from their wickedness under any circumstances, he said: 'These are my daughters (the women of the nation); do whatever you want to do with them (by marrying them), but for God's sake, do not disgrace me regarding my guests.' Under verse 78 of Surah Hud, we have clarified that this was not an offer from Lot, but was his final measure to shake the conscience of his nation. If these people had possessed any spark of moral sense, they could have thought: 'Here is a man who is ready to put even his own honor at risk to save the honor of his guests, and here we are seeking to violate his honor and the honor of his guests.' This is exactly like a man of high character who is ready to put his own life and dignity at risk to save another's life or honor.

'By your life, indeed they were in their intoxication wandering blindly.'

Completion of Proof and Punishment: When matters reached the point where even this final weapon of Lot had no effect on these intoxicated ones, Allah comforted him very affectionately: 'By your life, indeed they are wandering blindly in their intoxication.' The meaning is: now these are not people who will come to the path, so do not exhaust yourself over them. Now, only God's punishment will decide their fate.

'So the shriek seized them at sunrise.' 'Saihah' means a cry or shriek, and here it refers to that divine punishment which fell upon them at sunrise. Since this punishment—as detailed ahead—came in the form of thunder and lightning with a fierce wind, the word 'saihah' is highly appropriate for it.

'And We made its highest part its lowest and rained upon them stones of hard clay.'

The Nature of the Punishment: This is an elliptical indication of the punishment that befell the people of Lot. On the nature of this punishment, our teacher Maulana Farahi has conducted a detailed discussion in his commentary of Surah Ad-Dhariyat. We quote the necessary part of it here. Maulana's commentary is in Arabic; we present its translation: 'Allah sent a dust-laden wind upon the people of Lot which became fierce and ultimately turned into a "hasib" (a fierce wind pelting stones). First, a rain of pebbles and stones fell upon them, then it became so intense that by its force, their houses were turned upside down. Consequently, pointing to them, it was said: "And of them were those upon whom We sent a storm of stones." Also, He said: "So We made its highest part its lowest and rained upon them stones of hard clay, piled up." That is, such a fierce wind blew that their houses and roofs were leveled to the ground, and pebbles and sand covered them from above, as He said: "And the overthrown cities He overthrew, And covered them by that which covered."

Research on 'Sijjeel': Maulana Farahi has presented this research on the word 'sijjeel' in his commentary of Surah Al-Feel: 'Sijjeel' is arabized from two Persian words, 'sang' (stone) and 'gil' (mud). The Qur'an has explained it in two ways. In one place: 'and rained upon them stones of sijjeel,' meaning pebbles of the type of sijjeel. In another place: 'with stones of clay' (bi-hijaratim-min teen). Since this word had already become part of the Arabic language, the Qur'an used it.

Commentary on Verses 75 to 77: 'Indeed in that are signs for those who discern. And indeed, they [i.e., the ruins] are on an established road. Indeed in that is a sign for the believers.' 'Tawassum' means to discern, perceive, and obtain a lesson from a warning event. 'Sabeel muqeem' means a traveled road, a public highway.

A Lesson of Warning for the Quraysh: After narrating the story of the people of Lot, this first draws the attention of the disbelievers of the Quraysh: if they have eyes, let them obtain a lesson of warning from the destroyed towns which are not hidden from their eyes, but rather are situated on a traveled road which they frequently pass by. It is clear that the towns of Sodom and Gomorrah were situated on the trade route between Hijaz and Syria, and this route was the public highway for the trade caravans of both countries.

Then, specifically, attention is drawn to those who believed in the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)—showing that in this story there is also a great sign for them: although the people of truth face trials, ultimately they alone deserve God's mercy, and their enemies are crushed.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 78 to 96: In the following, there is a brief mention of some other nations who denied the messengers and the fate they faced. Then, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is consoled: remain firm on your call to truth; if the people of your nation also follow in the footsteps of their predecessor nations, they will face the same end that those nations faced. Do not be disheartened by their attitude, but overlook them gracefully. The success of the final end belongs to you and your companions alone. Read the verses:

'And the companions of the thicket [i.e., the people of Madyan] were wrongdoers.'

'Companions of the thicket' (Ashab-ul-Aykah) refers to the people of Madyan, to whom Shu'ayb (peace be upon him) was sent as a messenger. 'Aykah' means a thicket or forest. There was a large forest near Madyan, due to which these people were also known by this name. In this Surah, since the Quraysh are being drawn to those earthly signs of divine punishment which they frequently passed by during their trade journeys, the mention of the punished nations has come with reference to their locations and earthly signs. 'Wrongdoers' (zalimeen) here points to their polytheism, disbelief, and deviation from the straight path. In Qur'anic terminology, the greatest wrong is polytheism. Those who commit this wrong deny the greatest right of God (monotheism), wrong their own selves, and ultimately face that end which manifests for them from the Almighty as a punishment for this wrong.

'So We took retribution from them. And indeed, both [cities] are on a clear highway.' 'Retribution' (intiqam) here refers to the recompense of deeds that manifested for them after the completion of proof. In 'wa innahuma' (and indeed, both), the dual pronoun points to the town of the people of Lot alongside the companions of the thicket, both of which have been mentioned above—showing that the ruins and signs of both these towns are not hidden, but are situated on a clear highway which the caravans of the Arabs frequently pass. The meaning of 'imam mubeen' is exactly the same as 'sabeel muqeem' mentioned above: a clear highway, a public road, a traveled path. Since a path guides a traveler, the word 'imam' (leader/guide) is also used for it.

'And the companions of Al-Hijr [i.e., the people of Thamud] denied the messengers.' 'Al-Hijr' refers to the region between northern Arabia and Syria. This region was the dwelling place of the people of Thamud, among whom Salih (peace be upon him) was raised. We indicated above that in this Surah, the Quraysh are being drawn to those ruins and signs of punished nations which they frequently passed by. For this reason, the people of Thamud are also mentioned with reference to their dwelling place; the land of Lot, the land of Shu'ayb, and the region of Al-Hijr are all adjacent to one another and were the public highway for Arab caravans.

A Question and its Answer: A question might arise in someone's mind: the people of Thamud had denied only Salih (peace be upon him), so why is the plural word 'messengers' (Al-Mursaleen) used? The answer is that this expresses the intensity and gravity of their crime. All the messengers sent by God had the same call and the same message. For this reason, whoever denied any one of these messengers, in reality denied all of them.

'And We gave them Our signs, but they were from them turning away.' 'Signs' (Ayat) refers to both intellectual and natural proofs, as well as physical miracles. Among the physical miracles, the mention of the she-camel of Thamud has come in the Qur'an itself. It is possible that other miracles were also shown to them, but the Qur'an, instead of detailing them, has merely pointed to them, because the purpose here is only to show that all these proofs and miracles remained ineffective upon them.

'And they used to carve from the mountains, houses, feeling secure.' That is, closing their ears to the warning of the Prophet, they remained secure and untroubled, absorbed in their construction activities. They possessed perfection in the art of stone carving. They carved mountains to make highly stable and grand houses within them, and they took great pride in this art of theirs. How could the threat of the Prophet have any effect on the hearts of these people—that if divine punishment came, these very stable and grand houses would turn into graves for them?

Commentary on Verses 83 and 84: 'So the shriek seized them at early morning. And did not avail them what they used to earn.' That is, ultimately, what happened was what the Prophet had warned them of: one day divine punishment fell upon them, and none of their means and resources could avail them. The punishment that fell upon them is termed here as 'saihah' (shriek/cry). The reason for this, as we indicated above, is that Allah sent striped clouds upon Thamud, within which was a terrifying thunder and a deafening shriek.

Conclusion of the Surah and Consolation to the Prophet: 'And We have not created the heavens and the earth and that between them except in truth. And indeed, the Hour is coming; so overlook with gracious overlook.'

From here, the address turns directly to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), serving as the conclusion of the Surah, encompassing all its themes. Addressing you, it exhorts you to patience, steadfastness, and overlooking the opposition of the opponents gracefully. The introduction is: We have not created the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them aimlessly, in vain, or without a purpose; rather, We created them with a purpose and end. This world is not a playground of an amusement-seeker where people can do whatever they want without any accountability. The necessary requirement of its being purposeful is that a Day must come in which people are held accountable. Those who did good deeds should receive the reward of their goodness, and those who treated it as a playground to follow only their desires should face the punishment. Consequently, the Resurrection is bound to happen, and on that Day, those who are expending all their power in opposing you today will see their end. So, overlook their opposition and mischief gracefully. Graceful overlooking means that you should neither be disheartened or despairing because of their mischief, nor should you respond to their absurdities, nor should you abandon your duty of conveying and calling; rather, remain busy in your work and leave their matter to Allah. If they do not desist from their path, they too will face the same end that their predecessor nations faced.

'Indeed, your Lord - He is the Knowing Creator.' This is the proof of what was stated in the previous verse—that the Resurrection is bound to happen. It is said that your Lord is the Creator and is Knowing. The One Who created this entire world and is constantly creating, it is not difficult for Him to create people anew after their death. And He is Knowing—fully aware of every word and deed of everyone—so holding people to account for their words and deeds is not difficult for Him. Keep in view that the One Who is the Creator must naturally be fully aware of His creation: 'Does He Who created not know?' Meaning: can He Who created everyone be unaware of them?

A Source of Comfort and Consolation for the Prophet: 'And We have certainly given you seven of the often repeated [verses] and the great Quran.' This is a source of comfort, consolation, and a guarantee of ultimate success and victory, accompanying the exhortation to patience, steadfastness, and overlooking. It is said: We have granted you the seven oft-repeated verses and the great Qur'an. The meaning is: possessing this spiritual and angelic army, you are no longer in need of anything else to face your enemies and opponents. The God Who placed the responsibility of this great Qur'an upon you has promised that He will surely make you victorious: 'Indeed, He who imposed upon you the Quran will take you back to a place of return.'

What is Meant by 'Seven of the Oft-Repeated' (Sab'an minal-mathani): An important question arises here as to what is meant by 'Sab'an minal-mathani'. In response to this, various statements have been transmitted from the predecessors. One view is that it refers to the first seven long Surahs of the Qur'an. The second view is that it refers to Surah Al-Fatihah, which has seven verses including 'Bismillah', and which is repeated repeatedly in prayers. The third view is that it refers to the entire Qur'an. Although there are some other views, only these three are worthy of mention, and among them, the second view has gained the most fame. I have not seen any proof presented by its proponents in support of the first view. The sole proof presented by the proponents of the second view is that this Surah is repeated repeatedly in prayers. However, due to two points, this view does not satisfy the heart. First, the verses of Surah Al-Fatihah become seven only when 'Bismillah' is accepted as an integral part of it. Whether 'Bismillah' is an integral part of Al-Fatihah is a disputed matter, and the strong view is that 'Bismillah' is at the beginning of this Surah just as it is at the beginning of other Surahs. There is no specific reason to accept it as an integral part of Al-Fatihah. Second, the argument of being repeated repeatedly becomes valid only if 'mathani' means 'something repeated repeatedly'; but it actually means 'that which is in pairs'. This word has occurred in several places in the Qur'an, and in every place, it holds this meaning. For example: 'then marry those that please you of women, two (mathna) or three or four.' 'that you stand up for Allah, by twos (mathna) and singly, and then reflect.'

Although the third view has not gained much fame, in the light of proofs, it appears to carry more weight. The greatest proof in its favor is that the Qur'an itself explicitly states that the entire Qur'an is 'mathani': 'Allah has sent down the best statement as a consistent book of pairs (mathani).' Consistent (mutashabih) obviously means that all its parts are harmonious and of the same color—meaning that no contradiction or inconsistency is found in it.

As to the question of what is the meaning of its being 'mathani', the correct answer in our view is what we pointed out in the introduction of this book: all the Surahs of the Qur'an are in pairs. Every Surah has a partner (mathna) with it. We have presented Al-Baqarah and Aal-Imran from the long Surahs, and the Mu'awwidhatayn from the short Surahs as examples of this, and in this book of ours, we are constantly clarifying this reality while commenting on the Surahs.

Seven Groups of Surahs in the Qur'an: We have also clarified in great detail in the introduction that the arrangement of Surahs in the Qur'an is such that they form seven distinct groups. Every group begins with one or more Makki Surahs and ends with one or more Madani Surahs. In this way, the Qur'an comprises seven chapters within which the Surahs stand as sections. These chapters and sections share common themes, and every chapter and section also has a specific distinguishing aspect that differentiates them from one another. Surah Al-Fatihah stands as the preface of the entire Qur'an, in which all the themes detailed in the entire Qur'an have been stated in brief. It is as if within this gem, the entire city of meanings of the great Qur'an is enclosed. From this aspect, it is both 'seven of the oft-repeated' and the 'great Qur'an'.

'Do not extend your eyes toward that by which We have given enjoyment to [certain] categories of them, and do not grieve over them. And lower your wing to the believers.'

The word 'azwaj' (categories/partners) is also used in the sense of classes or groups. For example, in Surah Al-Waqi'ah: 'And you become three kinds (azwaj).' 

The meaning of the verse is: when God has granted you this great blessing of the Qur'an, which is the guarantee of your success and victory, you do not need to look at those who are intoxicated with the pride of their wealth and resources and are not ready to listen to and understand your word. Keep in view that the attention the prophets (peace be upon them) paid toward the wealthy and elite of their nations—particularly in the early period of the call—was not, God forbid, because they had any greed or value for their wealth and provisions in their eyes; rather, the sole cause was that if these influential and wealthy people believed, they would reform both their worldly life and the Hereafter, and their wealth and influence would also become a means of strengthening the religion and opening the path of religion for their subordinates. Out of this sincere desire, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) also paid special attention to the leaders of the Quraysh in the early period of the call; but when they did not open their eyes, Allah directed him to overlook them and focus all his attention and affection on those who, though lacking worldly wealth, possessed the blessing of faith in the great Qur'an. 'And do not grieve over them' shows that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was deeply grieved by the deprivation of these wealthy people from faith. Allah restricted him from this too—directing him not to grieve over their deprivation. These doomed people deserved to remain deprived of the blessing of faith in the Qur'an. 'And lower your wing to the believers' also contains a subtle indication: now there is no delay in the descent of divine wrath upon these doomed people, so just as a bird, upon sensing danger, hides its chicks under its wings, you similarly keep these believers under the cover of your affection.

'And say, "Indeed, I am the clear warner."' That is, announce to these arrogant and intoxicated ones: 'I am only a clear warner against the coming punishment and danger; so I have performed my duty and am performing it. As to whether you listened to my word or did not listen, the responsibility for that is not upon me, but upon yourselves.'

Commentary on Verses 90 and 91: 'Like We sent down on the dividers [i.e., the Jews and Christians] Who have made the Quran into portions.' This verse is connected with verse 87 above—showing that the entire statement is: We have granted you the seven oft-repeated verses and the great Qur'an just as We sent down Our word upon those who divided it into parts, making their scripture into pieces. This refers to the Jews, who—to hide the truth—also altered the arrangement of their scripture (the Torah) and divided it into various parts, hiding some and revealing others. This mischief of theirs has been mentioned in verse 92 of Surah Al-An'am. Precedents for using the word 'Qur'an' for other heavenly scriptures exist in the Qur'an itself; see verse 31 of Surah Ar-Ra'd. Incidentally, this verse also contains the answer to the Jews' objection as to what was the need for a new Qur'an after the Torah. The answer is: firstly, where does that scripture remain in its original form upon which they take pride—they have divided it into pieces; and if it had remained in its correct form, it would have itself provided the proof of the need for this new Qur'an.

'Ideen' is the plural of 'idah', which means divisions, pieces, and parts.

Commentary on Verses 92 and 93: 'So by your Lord, We will surely question them all About what they used to do.' Both these verses relate to the Jews. It is stated with a strong oath: those who committed these wrongs against the divine Book to hide the truth and for their personal interests and desires, We will surely hold them to account for their misdeeds one day.

'Then declare what you are commanded and turn away from the polytheists.' Now the speech connects back to its original sequence. The Prophet is instructed with emphasis: convey what you are commanded to its addressees without any decrease, fear, or hesitation, and pay no attention to the absurdities the polytheists are uttering against you.

Commentary on Verses 95 and 96: 'Indeed, We are sufficient for you against the mockers Who make [equal] with Allah another deity. But they are going to know.' That is, these polytheists who are mocking you and laughing at the warnings you convey to them, We are sufficient to deal with them on your behalf. They will soon know that every single word of what you are conveying to them has proven to be true.

Commentary on Verses 97 to 99: Exhortation to Patience and Steadfastness Against the Disbelievers' Sarcasm: 'And We already know that your breast is constrained by what they say. So exalt [Allah] with praise of your Lord and be of those who prostrate [to Him]. And worship your Lord until there comes to you the certainty.' This exhorts the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to patience against the sarcasm and mockery of the disbelievers, and shows him the way to obtain this patience and steadfastness: to glorify your Lord with praise, to maintain prayer with your believing companions, and to remain busy in the worship of your Lord until the morning of certainty dawns—meaning, every single matter of which you are informing people is proven to be a reality.

With these lines, the commentary of this Surah is completed. May Allah forgive all slips and make it beneficial for its readers. And our last call is that all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of the worlds.
